name: Cape Verdean Portuguese

ancestors.notes: European Portuguese colonial base. Cape Verde was settled by Portuguese from 1462; its Portuguese variety is among the oldest extra-peninsular varieties.

Cape Verdean Creole (Kriolu/Kabuverdianu) adstrate: the dominant vernacular of Cape Verde, spoken by virtually all Cape Verdeans as L1. Heavy bidirectional influence with Portuguese. Cape Verdean Creole is itself descended from Portuguese.

notes: Cape Verdean Portuguese (Português de Cabo Verde): the formal/L2 variety of Portuguese, the co-official language of Cape Verde. THIS IS DISTINCT FROM Cape Verdean Creole (Kabuverdianu/Kriolu, ISO kea): the Creole is the L1 vernacular of virtually the whole population, itself a Portuguese-lexified creole formed from 16th-17th-century Portuguese and West African languages that 'se diferenciava sobretudo com relação às vogais átonas' (Freitas 2017:154). This spec models the Portuguese, not the creole. GROUNDED / FLAGGED FEATURES: like other Lusophone-African emerging norms, the Portuguese of Cape Verde is reported to show (1) reduced-to-absent EP unstressed-vowel reduction (spelling-closer vocalism), (2) coda /s/ kept alveolar [s] rather than palatalised [ʃ], (3) an alveolar rather than uvular rhotic, (4) intervocalic /b d g/ kept as plosives rather than the EP spirants [β ð ɣ], and (5) a dental, non-velarised /l/. HONESTY LIMIT (hard rule 9): no accessible instrumental phonology of Cape Verdean PORTUGUESE (as opposed to the Creole) could be obtained and read for this pass — the CV-Portuguese feature list above rests on the general Lusophone-African pattern (cf. Undolo 2014 for Angola, read) plus the secondary Wikipedia description (flagged), while the read primary (Freitas 2017) covers the Creole. Features 1-5 are therefore provisional; this variety is deliberately kept at SKELETON tier (no read Cape-Verdean-Portuguese phonology source exists — the only variety-specific primary read, Freitas 2017, is about the Creole) and must not be promoted without a read CV-Portuguese source. Contrast pt-AO and pt-MZ, which are research tier because each has a read primary for its own variety (Undolo 2014, Nhatuve 2019). Two Creole macro-varieties (Sotavento/Santiago, Barlavento/São Vicente) underlie sub-regional variation.

sources.author: Baptista, M.
sources.title: The Syntax of Cape Verdean Creole

sources.author: Veiga, M.
sources.title: A construção do bilinguismo
sources.publisher: Instituto da Biblioteca Nacional e do Livro
sources.notes: Cape Verde bilingualism: Creole and Portuguese. Bibliographic reference (not read in full for this spec).

sources.author: Freitas, Shirley
sources.title: Kabuverdianu moderno: alguns aspectos fonológicos
sources.notes: Read. Describes the phonology of modern Kabuverdianu (Cape Verdean CREOLE, Santiago variety), used here to establish the Creole vs Portuguese distinction: the Creole formed from 16th-17th-century Portuguese and African languages, differing from it 'sobretudo com relação às vogais átonas' (p.154). NOT a source for Cape Verdean Portuguese phonology proper.

sources.author: Undolo, Márcio
sources.title: Caracterização do sistema vocálico do português culto falado em Angola
sources.notes: Read. Cited as the (Angolan) evidence for the shared Lusophone-African pattern of reduced-to-absent unstressed vowel reduction (p.183-184) by which the Cape Verdean Portuguese vocalism is modelled by analogy, in the absence of a read CV-Portuguese primary.

allophone_rules.notes: Cape Verdean Portuguese keeps an alveolar coda /s/ (no Lisbon 'chiado'); this overrides the inherited pt-PT PT_CODA_S_HUSH rule by id, matching the alveolar coda already declared in this spec's positional_graphemes.
Cape Verdean Portuguese keeps an alveolar coda /z/; overrides the inherited pt-PT PT_CODA_Z_HUSH rule by id.

stress.notes: Acordo Ortográfico 1990 conventions: paroxytone default; written accents override; final -i/-u/-r/-l/-z/-x/-im/-om/-um/-ns and nasal diphthongs attract final stress. Unmarked -em/-am stay paroxytone (homem, falam).
